Note that these risk differences and risk ratios are **conditional** on the covariate value $z$.
In many applications,
we want to summarize the effect across the population,
which requires computing a **marginal** risk difference or risk ratio.

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-observed-marginal-risk}

#### Observed marginal risk

The **observed marginal risk** under exposure level $A = a$ is:

$$\pi(a) \eqdef \Pr(Y = 1 \mid A = a)$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-observed-marginal-risk-direct}

#### Illustration: observed marginal risk

If 20% of the exposed group ($A = 1$) experience the outcome,
the observed marginal risk is $\pi(1) = \Pr(Y = 1 \mid A = 1) = 0.20$ —
simply the event proportion within that exposure group.

:::

This definition collapses the outcome over everything else in one step.
When the model also conditions on covariates $Z$,
we can unpack $\pi(a)$ as the covariate-specific risk $\pi(a, z)$
averaged over the distribution of $Z$ among those with $A = a$:

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_thm-observed-marginal-risk.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-observed-marginal-risk}

#### Observed marginal risk by standardization

Suppose a binary covariate $Z$ and, under exposure $A = 1$,
covariate-specific risks $\pi(1, 0) = 0.1$ and $\pi(1, 1) = 0.3$.
If half of the exposed have $Z = 1$
(so $\Pr(Z = 1 \mid A = 1) = 0.5$),
then @thm-observed-marginal-risk gives:

$$
\ba
\pi(1)
&= \E{\pi(1, Z) \mid A = 1}
&& \text{[observed marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= (0.5)(0.1) + (0.5)(0.3)
&& \text{[substitute stratum risks and weights]}
\\
&= 0.2
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\ea
$$

:::

In causal inference,
[potential outcomes](causal-inference.qmd#def-potential-outcomes) $Y^a$
represent the outcome each unit would have under exposure level $a$.
Population-level causal estimands summarize these potential outcomes:

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-potential-mean}

#### Potential mean

The **potential mean** (or **mean potential outcome**) under treatment $A = a$ is
the expected value of the potential outcome $Y^a$:

$$\mu_a \eqdef \E{Y^a}$$

The potential mean is what the average outcome would be
if the entire population were assigned treatment $a$.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-potential-mean}

#### Illustration: potential mean

If a treatment would produce the outcome in 20% of the population,
the potential mean is $\mu_a = \E{Y^a} = 0.2$.

:::

For a binary outcome,
the potential mean is a probability,
which we give its own name:

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-causal-marginal-risk}

#### Causal marginal risk

For a binary outcome $Y \in \{0, 1\}$,
the potential mean (@def-potential-mean) is a probability,
which we call the **causal marginal risk**
(also: **potential probability**, **potential marginal risk**)
under exposure level $a$:

$$\pi_a \eqdef \Pr(Y^a = 1)$$

:::

We use the term **causal marginal risk** throughout.

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-causal-marginal-risk-illustration}

#### Illustration: causal marginal risk

In a population where treatment $a$ would produce the outcome in 20% of people,
the causal marginal risk is $\pi_a = \Pr(Y^a = 1) = 0.20$ —
the fraction who would have the outcome if everyone were assigned treatment $a$.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#thm-causal-marginal-risk-equals-mean}

#### Causal marginal risk equals potential mean

The matching values in @exm-potential-mean and @exm-causal-marginal-risk-illustration
are no coincidence:
the causal marginal risk (@def-causal-marginal-risk)
is a special case of the potential mean (@def-potential-mean):

$$\pi_a = \E{Y^a}$$

::: {.proof}

Since $Y^a$ is binary ($Y^a \in \{0, 1\}$):

$$
\ba
\E{Y^a}
&= 1 \cdot \Pr(Y^a = 1) + 0 \cdot \Pr(Y^a = 0)
&& \text{[expectation of a binary variable]}
\\
&= \Pr(Y^a = 1)
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\\
&= \pi_a
&& \text{[definition of causal marginal risk]}
\ea
$$

:::

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-causal-marginal-risk-as-mean}

#### Illustration: causal marginal risk equals potential mean

With $\pi_a = 0.20$ as in @exm-causal-marginal-risk-illustration,
reading the potential mean as an expectation gives the same value:

$$\E{Y^a} = 1 \cdot 0.20 + 0 \cdot 0.80 = 0.20 = \pi_a.$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

Under causal identification assumptions
(see [Consistency](causal-inference.qmd#def-consistency),
[Conditional Exchangeability](causal-inference.qmd#def-exchangeability),
and [Positivity](causal-inference.qmd#def-positivity)),
the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$ (@def-causal-marginal-risk)
can be expressed in terms of observed data:

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_thm-causal-marginal-risk.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-causal-marginal-risk}

#### Causal marginal risk by standardization

Continuing @exm-observed-marginal-risk
with the same covariate-specific risks
$\pi(1, 0) = 0.1$ and $\pi(1, 1) = 0.3$,
suppose only 20% of the whole population has $Z = 1$
($\Pr(Z = 1) = 0.2$),
so the exposed are enriched for the high-risk level.
Then @thm-causal-marginal-risk gives:

$$
\ba
\pi_1
&= \E{\pi(1, Z)}
&& \text{[causal marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= (0.8)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.3)
&& \text{[substitute stratum risks and weights]}
\\
&= 0.14
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\ea
$$

This differs from the observed marginal risk $\pi(1) = 0.2$
because the exposed subgroup's covariate distribution
differs from the population's.

:::

We can now place the observed and causal marginal risks side by side:

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_rem-observed-vs-causal-marginal-risk.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#cor-observed-equals-causal}

#### Observed and causal marginal risks coincide without confounding

Under the identification assumptions of @thm-causal-marginal-risk
([Consistency](causal-inference.qmd#def-consistency),
[Conditional Exchangeability](causal-inference.qmd#def-exchangeability),
and [Positivity](causal-inference.qmd#def-positivity)),
if additionally $Z \perp A$ (as under complete randomization of $A$),
the observed and causal marginal risks are equal:

$$\pi(a) = \pi_a$$

::: {.proof}

When $Z \perp A$ the conditional and marginal distributions of $Z$ coincide,
$\red{f_{Z \mid A}(z \mid a)} = \red{f_Z(z)}$,
so @thm-observed-marginal-risk and @thm-causal-marginal-risk give:

$$
\ba
\pi(a)
&= \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)} \mid A = a}
&& \text{[observed marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}}
&& \text{[no confounding]}
\\
&= \pi_a
&& \text{[causal marginal risk theorem]}
\ea
$$

:::

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-observed-equals-causal}

#### Illustration: coincidence under randomization

Take the covariate-specific risks $\pi(1, 0) = 0.1$ and $\pi(1, 1) = 0.3$
of @exm-observed-marginal-risk, but now suppose $A$ is randomized,
so the exposed carry the population's covariate distribution,
$\Pr(Z = 1 \mid A = 1) = \Pr(Z = 1) = 0.2$.
Then the observed marginal risk

$$
\ba
\pi(1)
&= \E{\pi(1, Z) \mid A = 1}
&& \text{[observed marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= (0.8)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.3)
&& \text{[substitute randomized weights]}
\\
&= 0.14
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\ea
$$

equals the causal marginal risk $\pi_1 = 0.14$ of @exm-causal-marginal-risk,
exactly as @cor-observed-equals-causal predicts.

:::

Two related procedures estimate these marginal risks from a fitted regression model:

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-g-computation}

#### G-computation

**G-computation** (also called the **g-formula** or **standardization**)
is a procedure for estimating the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$
(@def-causal-marginal-risk) from observed data
[@robins1986new; see also @hernan2020causal, Chapter 13]:

<!-- Reviewer note (verified, do not re-flag): in Hernán & Robins,
"Causal Inference: What If" (single edition, 2020), Chapter 13 is
"Standardization and the parametric g-formula"; Chapter 14 is
"G-Estimation of Structural Nested Models". The Chapter 13 locator is
correct for g-computation. -->

1. Fit a regression model for $\hat\pi(a, z) = \widehat{\Pr}(Y = 1 \mid A = a, Z = z)$
2. For each observation $i$, predict the risk under exposure level $a$,
   writing $\hat\pi_{a,i} \eqdef \hat\pi(a, z_i)$ for short
3. Average these predicted risks over the observed covariate distribution:

$$\hat\pi_a = \frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi_{a,i}$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#thm-g-computation-consistency}

#### Consistency of g-computation

Suppose the outcome regression is correctly specified,
so the fitted conditional risk is consistent for the true conditional risk,
$\hat\pi(a, z) \overset{p}{\to} \teal{\pi(a, z)}$ for each $z$,
and suppose the identification assumptions of @thm-causal-marginal-risk hold.
Then the g-computation estimator (@def-g-computation)
is consistent for the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$ (@def-causal-marginal-risk):

$$\hat\pi_a \overset{p}{\to} \pi_a$$

::: {.proof}

Split the estimator into the average of the **true** conditional risks
plus an estimation-error term:

$$
\ba
\hat\pi_a
&= \frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi(a, z_i)
&& \text{[definition of the estimator]}
\\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sumin \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}
+ \frac{1}{n}\sumin \left(\hat\pi(a, z_i) - \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}\right)
&& \text{[add and subtract the true risks]}
\ea
$$

The estimation-error term converges to zero in probability:
under correct specification the fitted coefficients are consistent,
$\hat\beta \overset{p}{\to} \beta^*$,
and since each fitted risk is a bounded, continuous function of $\hat\beta$,
the continuous mapping theorem gives
$\frac{1}{n}\sumin \left|\hat\pi(a, z_i) - \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}\right| \overset{p}{\to} 0$
(pointwise convergence of the individual terms would not suffice on its own,
since they are correlated through the shared $\hat\beta$).
The remaining term averages the true risks over an i.i.d. sample
$z_1, \dots, z_n$ from the marginal distribution $f_Z$,
so the weak law of large numbers gives

$$
\frac{1}{n}\sumin \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}
\overset{p}{\to} \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}}.
\qquad \text{[weak law of large numbers]}
$$

Finally, @thm-causal-marginal-risk identifies this expectation
with the causal marginal risk:

$$
\E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}} = \pi_a.
\qquad \text{[causal marginal risk identification]}
$$

Combining the three steps, $\hat\pi_a \overset{p}{\to} \pi_a$.

:::

:::

The following example carries out this procedure on the WCGS data.

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_exm-wcgs-causal-marginal-risks.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-predictive-margins}

#### Predictive margins

A **predictive margin** is the fitted risk $\hat\pi(a, z)$
averaged over a chosen set of observations $S$
[the term is due to @graubard1999predictive;
@lane1982analysis develop the underlying standardization-as-prediction framework]:

$$
\widehat{\text{PM}}(a \mid S) \eqdef \frac{1}{|S|} \sum_{i \in S} \hat\pi_{a,i}
$$

using the shorthand $\hat\pi_{a,i} = \hat\pi(a, z_i)$ from @def-g-computation,
where $|S|$ is the number of observations in $S$.
The choice of the averaging set $S$ determines which estimand the margin targets;
the two standard choices each have their own name.
The term "predictive margins" is commonly used in social science
and in Stata's `margins` command.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-full-sample-predictive-margin}

#### Full-sample predictive margin

The **full-sample predictive margin** averages the fitted risks
over the **whole sample** $S = \{1, \dots, n\}$:

$$
\widehat{\text{PM}}(a) \eqdef \frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi_{a,i} = \hat\pi_a
$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#cor-full-sample-pm}

#### Full-sample predictive margin estimates the causal marginal risk

The full-sample predictive margin is consistent
for the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$ (@def-causal-marginal-risk):

$$\widehat{\text{PM}}(a) = \hat\pi_a \overset{p}{\to} \pi_a$$

::: {.proof}
The full-sample predictive margin $\frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi_{a,i}$
is, term for term, the g-computation estimator $\hat\pi_a$ of @def-g-computation,
so its consistency for $\pi_a$ is exactly @thm-g-computation-consistency.
:::

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-full-sample-pm}

#### Illustration: full-sample predictive margin

In the WCGS example (@exm-wcgs-causal-marginal-risks),
the g-computation estimate $\hat\pi_1$ is simultaneously
the full-sample predictive margin $\widehat{\text{PM}}(1)$:
both are the same average $\frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi_{1,i}$
of the fitted Type A risks over the whole sample,
so the margin inherits its consistency for $\pi_1$ from @cor-full-sample-pm.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-subgroup-predictive-margin}

#### Exposed-subgroup predictive margin

The **exposed-subgroup predictive margin** averages the fitted risks
only over the observations actually exposed to $A = a$,
$S = \{i : A_i = a\}$,
and we give it its own symbol $\hat\pi_a(a)$:

$$
\hat\pi_a(a) \eqdef \frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{i : A_i = a} \hat\pi_{a,i}
$$

where $n_a$ is the number of observations with $A_i = a$.
The subscript records the treatment level the risks are predicted under,
and the argument records the subgroup averaged over;
the full-sample margin $\hat\pi_a$ averages over everyone instead.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#thm-subgroup-pm}

#### Exposed-subgroup predictive margin estimates the observed marginal risk

Under correct model specification,
the exposed-subgroup predictive margin is consistent
for the observed marginal risk $\pi(a)$ (@def-observed-marginal-risk):

$$\hat\pi_a(a) \overset{p}{\to} \pi(a)$$

::: {.proof}

Split the estimator into the average of the **true** conditional risks
over the exposed subgroup plus an estimation-error term:

$$
\ba
\hat\pi_a(a)
&= \frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{i : A_i = a} \hat\pi(a, z_i)
&& \text{[definition of the estimator]}
\\
&= \frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{i : A_i = a} \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}
+ \frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{i : A_i = a} \left(\hat\pi(a, z_i) - \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}\right)
&& \text{[add and subtract the true risks]}
\ea
$$

The estimation-error term converges to zero in probability
under correct model specification.
The observations with $A_i = a$ are an i.i.d. sample
from the conditional distribution $f_{Z \mid A}(z \mid a)$,
so the weak law of large numbers gives

$$
\frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{i : A_i = a} \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}
\overset{p}{\to} \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)} \mid A = a}.
\qquad \text{[weak law of large numbers]}
$$

Finally, @thm-observed-marginal-risk identifies this conditional expectation
with the observed marginal risk:

$$
\E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)} \mid A = a} = \pi(a).
\qquad \text{[observed marginal risk theorem]}
$$

Combining the three steps, $\hat\pi_a(a) \overset{p}{\to} \pi(a)$.

:::

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-subgroup-pm}

#### Illustration: exposed-subgroup predictive margin

Continuing @exm-observed-marginal-risk,
where the exposed ($A = 1$) have $\Pr(Z = 1 \mid A = 1) = 0.5$
and covariate-specific risks $\pi(1, 0) = 0.1$ and $\pi(1, 1) = 0.3$,
averaging the fitted risks over the exposed subgroup gives

$$
\hat\pi_1(1) = (0.5)(0.1) + (0.5)(0.3) = 0.2,
$$

which estimates the observed marginal risk $\pi(1) = 0.2$ (@exm-observed-marginal-risk),
not the causal marginal risk $\pi_1 = 0.14$ (@exm-causal-marginal-risk).

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#lem-subgroup-pm-proportion}

#### Exposed-subgroup predictive margin reproduces the observed proportion

If the fitted model includes the exposure $A$ as a categorical covariate,
the exposed-subgroup predictive margin equals
the observed event proportion in that subgroup **exactly**,
in any finite sample:

$$
\hat\pi_a(a) = \bar Y_a
\eqdef \frac{1}{n_a} \sum_{i : A_i = a} Y_i
$$

::: {.proof}

The maximum-likelihood score equations for a logistic GLM
set $\sum_{i} x_{ij} (Y_i - \hat\mu_i) = 0$ for each covariate column $x_{\cdot j}$,
where $\hat\mu_i = \hat\pi(A_i, z_i)$ is observation $i$'s fitted risk.
Because $A$ enters as a categorical covariate,
the indicator column for level $a$ contributes the score equation

$$
\sum_{i : A_i = a} (Y_i - \hat\mu_i) = 0,
\quad\text{i.e.}\quad
\sum_{i : A_i = a} \hat\mu_i = \sum_{i : A_i = a} Y_i.
$$

(For the reference level, which has no indicator column of its own,
the same identity follows by subtracting the non-reference indicator
score equations from the intercept score equation
$\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \hat\mu_i) = 0$.)

For an observation with $A_i = a$ we have $\hat\mu_i = \hat\pi(a, z_i)$,
so dividing by $n_a$ gives
$\hat\pi_a(a) = \frac{1}{n_a}\sum_{i : A_i = a}\hat\pi(a, z_i)
= \frac{1}{n_a}\sum_{i : A_i = a} Y_i = \bar Y_a$.

:::

:::

The following example applies these predictive margins to the same WCGS data.

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_exm-wcgs-observed-marginal-risks.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_sec-marginal-risk-differences.qmd >}}
